coredata.dc:title: A comprehensive study of the thermophysical and rheological properties of ZrO2 based nanofluids as geothermal fluids

coredata.dc:description: Geothermal heat pump systems in residential and commercial applications have become popular in many countries over the past years. The heat transfer performance of the ground heat exchangers in these systems has still room for improvement since they have huge influence on the overall efficiency. Likewise, new heat transfer fluids with enhanced properties, known as nanofluids, have been proposed as a potential solution to substitute the conventional working fluids and to improve the heat transfer processes and performance. A reliable and appropriated proposal of nanofluids for a particular application must include a complete fluid dynamic characterization including thermophysical, rheological, heat transfer coefficients, and pressure drops analysis, as well as physical or chemical characterization of the nanomaterial. In this study, a novel proposal of propylene glycol:water (10:90 vol%)-based zirconium oxide nanofluids of different nanoparticle mass concentrations (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, and 5.0 wt%) as possible geothermal working fluids and their thermophysical and rheological characterization are performed. Thus, the nanopowder was extensively investigated by means of Transmission Electron Microscopy, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy, X-Ray diffraction, and Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy obtaining the shape, size distribution, d-spacing, electron diffraction pattern, and crystallinity. Then, thermal conductivities, dynamic viscosities, densities, and isobaric heat capacities for base fluid and nanofluids were measured by transient hot wire, rotational rheometry, vibrating tube, and differential scanning calorimetry methods, respectively. Increases in thermal conductivity, dynamic viscosity, and density of the nanofluids up to 2.8%, 13%, and 4.1% were found, respectively, while decreases in heat capacity reached 11% in comparison to the base fluid. Different models and equations were also employed to analyse the experimental data.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Geothermal heat pump systems in residential and commercial applications have become popular in many countries over the past years. The heat transfer performance of the ground heat exchangers in these systems has still room for improvement since they have huge influence on the overall efficiency. Likewise, new heat transfer fluids with enhanced properties, known as nanofluids, have been proposed as a potential solution to substitute the conventional working fluids and to improve the heat transfer processes and performance. A reliable and appropriated proposal of nanofluids for a particular application must include a complete fluid dynamic characterization including thermophysical, rheological, heat transfer coefficients, and pressure drops analysis, as well as physical or chemical characterization of the nanomaterial. In this study, a novel proposal of propylene glycol:water (10:90 vol%)-based zirconium oxide nanofluids of different nanoparticle mass concentrations (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, and 5.0 wt%) as possible geothermal working fluids and their thermophysical and rheological characterization are performed. Thus, the nanopowder was extensively investigated by means of Transmission Electron Microscopy, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy, X-Ray diffraction, and Ultraviolet visible spectroscopy obtaining the shape, size distribution, d-spacing, electron diffraction pattern, and crystallinity. Then, thermal conductivities, dynamic viscosities, densities, and isobaric heat capacities for base fluid and nanofluids were measured by transient hot wire, rotational rheometry, vibrating tube, and differential scanning calorimetry methods, respectively. Increases in thermal conductivity, dynamic viscosity, and density of the nanofluids up to 2.8%, 13%, and 4.1% were found, respectively, while decreases in heat capacity reached 11% in comparison to the base fluid. Different models and equations were also employed to analyse the experimental data.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The awareness of the energy crisis that the world is facing has encouraged the use of renewable energies like solar, biomass or geothermal energy in residential and commercial environments. Among them, geothermal heat pump (GHP) systems have gained popularity and made the most significant impact on the direct-use of geothermal energy in 58 countries and regions in 2020, China, USA, Sweden, Germany, and Finland being the leading countries in terms of installed capacity [1]. The thermal stability of the ground, the maintenance cost reduction of GHP systems in comparison to conventional ones, and their high Coefficient of Performance (COP) have made ground-source heat pumps a leading choice for the heating and cooling of buildings [2,3]. Additionally, geothermal energy is environment-friendly, contributes to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, and is less influenced by the weather conditions than other renewable energies such as wind or solar.

Deep aquifers, conductive systems, and convective (hydrothermal) systems are the different types of geothermal resources. Among them, convective systems can be classified as high, medium, and low enthalpy resources according to their temperature. It is considered as a high enthalpy resource when the geothermal fluid is at T greater than 423 K (150 °C), medium enthalpy resource when 384 K < T < 423 K, and low enthalpy resource when T less than 384 K (111 °C). Volcanoes and geysers are generally known as sources of high enthalpy geothermal energy while low enthalpy geothermal energy is stored in rocks on the Earth’s crust [4]. Over the years, high and medium enthalpy geothermal resources have been used for electricity production [5], the latest by using techniques such as Organic Rankine Cycle and Kalina Cycle [6]. Low enthalpy resources have a great potential for electricity generation as well since these reservoirs are widespread and occur at shallow depths [7]. However, they have been used in other important applications, mainly for heating systems working directly through geothermal heat pumps.

A geothermal heat pump system consists of three main parts: ground heat exchanger (GHE), heat pump, and heat distribution subsystem. The GHE is the one of the main components, since it exchanges heat with the ground using it as a heat sink during summer or a heat source in winter. Its thermal performance has a noteworthy influence in the global energy efficiency of the system [8]. GHEs can be classified based on their circulating fluid, loop types (closed or open), position (horizontal, vertical, or oblique), pipe connections (series or parallel), among others. [9]. Regarding the loop types, closed loops are relatively isolated systems and have several advantages: since no water circulates in the reservoir, water contaminants and the permeability of the reservoir are not considered; there is no risk of groundwater pollution; and the additional costs related to the reservoir stimulation projects are reduced [10]. In a closed loop GHE, heat exchange occurs between the ground and a fluid circulating through a tube in a closed circuit [11]. The performance of the GHE is influenced by the selected working fluid. A variety of fluids has been used such as pure water [12,13], mixtures of glycol and water [14,15], ethanol, or ethanol and water mixtures [16,17]. Pure water (W) is the most used in GHEs because of its high thermal conductivity, its availability, and the existing knowledge about its thermal and fluid-dynamic behaviour. However, the addition of glycols is frequently required to reduce the freezing point of water and prevent damage to installations and heat exchangers. Among the different glycols available as secondary fluids, propylene glycol (PG), i.e., 1,2 propanediol, is an interesting choice due to the concern over the toxicity of other extensively used glycols, especially ethylene glycol-based products. In geothermal collection, aqueous solutions of propylene glycol at 10–30 wt% are typically used [18]. However, the addition of glycols to pure water affects the entire thermophysical profile of the mixture lowering the resulting thermal conductivity of the fluid and hindering the heat transfer performance. Thus, nanofluids have been proposed as a potential solution to overcome this limitation. There are only a few experimental studies of the effect of nanofluids on the thermal performance of GHEs. Du et al. [19] experimentally analysed a 3 wt% CuO/water nanofluid in a GHE, finding increases of 39.8% and 16.6% of the heat transfer rate and the pumping power consumption, respectively. Kapıcıoğlu and Esen [20] experimentally studied the performance of two different volume concentrations of Al2O3/ethylene glycol:water nanofluids (0.1, and 0.2%) in horizontal U-type and spiral GHE. A performance increase of 19% and 21% of the 0.1% nanofluid compared to the base fluid in the U-type and spiral GHE were found, respectively. Moreover, an increase of the COP close to 2.5% and 3% in U-type and spiral GHE were achieved by using 0.1% nanofluid, respectively.

Likewise, it is necessary to propose more nanofluids based on propylene glycol:water mixtures at low glycol concentrations and analyse their feasibility as new geothermal fluids. In particular, there is a lack of literature about those based on PG:W 10:90 vol% mixture (frost protection up to 270.38 K [27,28]) that could be a prospective solution to improve the heat transfer performance of GHEs and, consequently, the COP of a GHP system. Therefore, a thorough analysis of these nanofluids is fundamental before their use in real installations. The accurate determination of their thermophysical profile will allow to carry out experimental analyses of heat transfer performance and then, selecting the most suitable nanofluid for each GHE. In this study, a comprehensive study of zirconia (ZrO2) nanofluids of different mass concentrations (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, and 5.0 wt%) based on a PG:W binary mixture at 10:90 vol%, ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids, is carried out. Firstly, a characterization was made by means of Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) to properly analyse the morphology and crystallinity of the nanopowder. Afterwards, the thermal conductivity, dynamic viscosity, density, and isobaric heat capacity were experimentally determined for all ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids and were compared to those of the base fluid. Additionally, the reliability of several widely used theoretical models for the prediction or correlation of our thermophysical data was assessed.

Zirconium oxide (ZrO2) nanoparticles, obtained from IoLiTec (Ionic Liquids Technologies GmbH, Germany) with an average particle size of 30–60 nm, 3 mol% yttrium oxide stabilized, and 99.9% purity were dispersed in a propylene glycol(PG):water(W) 10:90 vol% mixture. Propylene glycol was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, United States), with a commercial mass purity of 99.5%, while water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm at 298.15 K was obtained through a Milli-Q 185 Plus System from Millipore (Watford, United Kingdom). Agar powder purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, Missouri, USA) was used as a gelling agent to prevent convection during the thermal conductivity measurement. Samples were weighed by using a Sartorius CPA225 analytical balance (Götitingen, Germany) whose accuracy is 1·10-5 g and dispersed in an ultrasonic homogenizer Bandelin Sonoplus HD 2200 (Berlin, Germany).

Five different mass concentrations of ZrO2 in a PG:W mixture at 10:90 vol% were prepared following a two-step method. Table 1 summarizes the studied nanofluids with the corresponding nanoparticle volume and mass fractions.

The dispersions were sonicated by using the aforementioned ultrasonic probe during a total time of 45 min at a power of 60 W and an operating frequency of 20 kHz, first 30 min with the nanoparticles dispersed in the propylene glycol and 15 min more after adding the water.

The characterization of the nanopowder, base fluid, and the proposed nanofluids, as well as the study of their thermophysical properties were performed by a set of experimental techniques. The morphology and size of ZrO2 nanoparticles were analysed via Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEOL JEM 1010 (Tokyo, Japan) and a field emission JEOL JEM2010 FEG (Tokyo, Japan), working at operating accelerator voltages of 100 kV and 200 kV, respectively. Images of the studied nanoparticles were obtained, as well as their electron diffraction patterns by Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) image mode. The phase, purity, and crystallinity of ZrO2 nanoparticles were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests by means of a XPERT-PRO diffractometer (Malvern, United Kingdom) with Cu Kα monochromatic radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at 30 mA and an operational voltage of 40 kV. The crystalline domain size, D, of ZrO2 was estimated through the Scherrer equation [29]:
(1)

K Λ
β c o s [ 03 B 8 ]
where K is the Scherrer constant typically considered to be 0.9 for spherical crystals,
Λ
is the X-ray wavelength,
β
is the width of the diffraction peak at half its height, and
θ
the particular diffraction angle of the peak. UV–vis characterization was also performed to obtain useful information about the molecular bonds and chemical structure of the designed nanofluids by means of a Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, California, United States). Structural analysis of the propylene glycol-based zirconia nanofluids was performed through a Fourier-Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy technique by using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, United States) equipped with an attenuated total reflection (ATR) attachment. The spectra of the samples were recorded within the wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The knowledge of the nanoparticle-fluid interactions might help understanding the reasons behind the changes in physical and transport properties of the proposed nanofluids.

The stability of the dispersions was assessed by determining the zeta potential of the low concentrated sample (0.25 wt% nanofluid) at 293.15 K using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, United Kingdom). The thermal conductivities of both base fluid and nanofluids were obtained by means of a KD2 Pro thermal analyzer (Decagon Devices, Pullman, United States) based on the transient hot wire method (THW) by using the KS-1 probe over the temperature range from 283.15 to 333.15 K with an uncertainty (k = 2) of 5% [30]. The thermal stability of the samples was achieved by submerging them into a Polyscience 9510 (Illinois, United States) thermostatic bath at constant temperature during the measurements. The formation of convection is fostered by the high heat capacity of the used probe compared to the traditional platinum wires used for THW devices [31]. Therefore, agar powder was used as a gelling agent to prevent this convection during the thermal conductivity measurements [31]. To validate the reliability of agar powder as stabilising agent for thermal conductivity measurements using the KD2 Pro thermal analyser, thermal conductivity of PG:W mixture stabilized with agar was measured within the temperature range from 283.15 to 333.15 K and compared to literature values reported by Melinder [28] and ASHRAE [32]. To compose the gel, ∼14 ml of the sample was heated at 403 K for 15–20 min and then 0.50 wt% of agar was added while stirring at 1000 rpm until a homogeneous solution was observed. Afterwards, the fluid was slightly cooled before performing any measurement.

A rheological characterization was performed through a Physica MCR 101 rotational rheometer (Anton Paar, Graz, Austria) with a viscosity uncertainty (k = 2) of 5%, equipped with a cone-plate geometry CP50-1 (50 mm diameter and 1° cone angle) and a Peltier system in the plate [33]. A 102 μm gap between the geometry and the Peltier was maintained constant for all tests.

Experimental densities of all samples were determined through the oscillating U-tube technique by using a DMA 501 density meter (Anton Paar, Graz, Austria), uncertainty (k = 2) of 0.1%, over the temperature range from 288.15 to 313.15 K [34]. Finally, isobaric heat capacities of the designed nanofluids and base fluid were assessed by means of a TA Instruments Q2000 (New Castle, United States) heat-flux differential scanning calorimeter equipped with a RSC90 cooling system through a quasi-isothermal temperature-modulated differential scanning calorimetry method (TMDSC), uncertainty (k = 2) of 3% [35]. Samples were kept in hermetically sealed Tzero aluminium pans and tests were carried out in a temperature range from 283.15 to 333.15 K.

As mentioned before, the ZrO2 nanopowder was analysed through TEM and HRTEM. Images from TEM confirmed a pseudo-spherical shape and a possible appearance of cluster formation of the nanoparticles, as it can be observed in Fig. 1 a. The size distribution of the ZrO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 1b) was achieved by measuring more than 200 nanoparticles from TEM images, obtaining a mean particle size of 26*/2 nm and a polydispersity index of 0.22, assuming a log-normal distribution function [36]. Fig. 1c shows a HRTEM image of ZrO2 nanoparticles where different layers of the crystalline structure with a d-spacing of 3.54 Å corresponding to the distance between (100) planes of tetragonal ZrO2 can be noted. A ring pattern by Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED), typical of polycrystalline materials, is displayed in Fig. 1d, where (101), (002), (112), and (103) planes of the tetragonal structure of ZrO2 can be identified. The analysis of the diffraction patterns was performed with the GMS3 software and the results were compared to the literature [37–40] concluding that they corresponded to the mentioned crystalline structure.

The crystallinity of ZrO2 nanoparticles was analysed through XRD technique. Zirconium dioxide possesses polymorphism with three different crystalline modifications; the monoclinic which is stable up to 1473 K, the tetragonal which is stable up to 2173 K, and the cubic which is stable above 2173 K [41]. Fig. 2 depicts the obtained diffraction pattern where Bragg reflections with 2θ values of 30.25, 35.21, 50.24, and 60.96° corresponding to (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively, for the tetragonal structure of ZrO2 and 2θ values of 35.21, 50.24, and 60.96° corresponding to (002), (022), and (113) planes, respectively, for the monoclinic structure of ZrO2 can be observed. Although the monoclinic structure was also detected in the XRD spectrum, all the intense peaks correspond to planes of the tetragonal structure of zirconia. Considering the results obtained from the XRD analysis and the fact that the d-spacing determined from HRTEM images correspond to a plane of the tetragonal structure, we conclude that the crystalline structure of this nanopowder corresponds to the tetragonal structure of ZrO2. A crystalline domain (D) size of 22.75 nm was determined from the (111) reflection of the tetragonal structure, being also the most intense peak of the spectrum, through Equation 1.

UV–Vis spectrum of a diluted ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% dispersion is shown in Fig. 3 where a peak at a wavelength of ∼ 210 nm can be observed. According to Gutiérrez-Alejandre et al. [42] and Chen et al. [43], this main peak is likely due to O2— → Zr4 + charge transfer transitions. There is an excitation of the electrons from the valence band (O 2p character) to the conduction band (Zr 4d character).

Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectrum providing information about the chemical bonds in ZrO2/PG nanofluids. The peaks corresponding to the stretching of CH3 groups can be observed at 2970 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching) and 2876 cm−1 (symmetric stretching) and those of CH2 groups at 2930 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching). The absorption bands attributed to the bending of CH3 and CH2 groups appear at 1458 cm−1 (CH3 asymmetric bending + CH2 scissoring) and 1376 cm−1 (CH3 symmetric bending). The broad peak at 3313 cm−1 indicates the OH stretching vibration. The C-OH stretching vibration is observed at 1038 cm−1 (primary OH) and at 1136 cm−1 (secondary OH), whereas the C-OH torsion vibration peak appears at 657 cm−1. The findings of the experimental spectra of all ZrO2/PG nanofluids fit with the spectrum of propylene glycol, meaning that there are no chemical bonds between the nanoparticles and the base fluid molecules. However, the nanoparticle loading modifies the transmittance of the dispersion, and it also causes a slightly shift of the broad peak that indicates the OH stretching vibration, the higher the nanoparticle loading, the bigger the shift. Taking the 3313 cm−1 peak of 0.25 wt% ZrO2/PG nanofluid as the reference, shifts up to 1.1% to the left were found for the 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG nanofluid. According to Suganthi et al. [44], the ratio of intensities of CH2 stretching and C-OH stretching bands in relation to intensity of OH stretching vibrations of free and hydrogen bonded can be used as measure of the strength of hydrogen bonds. Lower values of the intensity ratios, I CH2/I OH and I CH2/I C-OH, indicates intermolecular weakening of hydrogen bond. In the case of ZrO2/PG:W nanofluids, these ratios increase with the nanoparticle loading, which entails more potential intermolecular hydrogen bonds.

The zeta potential can be defined as the charge that is established at the interface between the surface of a colloidal particle and the liquid in which it is dispersed. It is often claimed as one of the fundamental parameters that account for stability. The nanofluid literature often states that absolute zeta potential values above 30 mV are a symptom of moderate stability [45]. The 0.25 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid showed a zeta potential of 44.8 mV, evidencing a good stability according to the mentioned criterium.

Thermal conductivity, λ, of PG:W 10:90 vol% mixture was measured within the temperature range from 283.15 to 333.15 K. A significant scatter of the experimental values with the increasing temperature was found, likely due to the decrease of the binary mixture viscosity that allows convection currents to form, as it was reported by Ebrahimi et al. [31] for deionised water. In order to limit this scattering of the thermal conductivity values, 0.50 wt% mass fraction of agar powder was added to the PG:W mixture to perform the measurements. The gel formed with the addition of agar limits the convection at higher temperatures, which makes the measurement more accurate when using transient hot wire method and decreased the scattering of the experimental data.

A comparison with literature values reported by Melinder [28] and ASHRAE [32] was carried out. The resulting data for PG:W 10:90 vol% without agar are plotted in Fig. 5 and it can be noted that two of the values fall outside the 5% of the reference ones, showing maximum absolute deviations up to 8.8% with regard to ASHRAE values [32] and 10% comparing to Melinder values [28]. Regarding the values obtained for PG:W 10:90 vol% mixture with 0.50 wt% mass fraction of agar, it can be observed that all the data-points are within the 5% of reference values for the mentioned binary mixture, obtaining maximum absolute deviations up to 2.0 and 0.90% comparing to ASHRAE [32] and Melinder values [28], respectively. In addition, since the experimental data of PG:W 10:90 vol% with agar show low deviations compared to the literature, it can be assumed that the addition of 0.50 wt% mass fraction of agar does not entail a significant change on the thermal conductivity of the binary mixture, and therefore, the PG:W-based nanofluids. Consequently, this stabilized agent has no other effect than preventing convection during thermal conductivity measurements according to the literature [31].

Accordingly, agar was incorporated in the preparation of the studied nanofluids to perform thermal conductivity measurements from 283.15 to 333.15 K with a 10 K temperature step. The highest enhancement in thermal conductivity correspond to 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid with average increases of 2.8% compared to the base fluid. Fig. 6 a depicts the experimental thermal conductivity of the propylene glycol:water mixture and all ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids. The same increasing trend of the thermal conductivity of nanofluids with the increasing temperature compared to that of PG:W 10:90 vol% can be observed, obtaining λ values 10% higher at 333.15 K compared to those at 283.15 K for all samples. Another widely used model is that proposed by Xue et al. [50], which is a modification of Maxwell model that accounts the effect of nanoadditive volume concentrations. Although this model was designed for multi-walled-carbon nanotube composites, the

Experimental thermal conductivity of the proposed nanofluids and that obtained through the three models mentioned above are shown in Fig. 6b. As can be seen, the predicted values underestimate the thermal conductivity of ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids and the higher the concentration of ZrO2 nanoparticles, the more noticeable are these differences. Moreover, these predictive values exhibit a linear behaviour with the volume (or mass) fraction of dispersed nanoparticles in the studied concentration range, whilst the experimental results show non-linear dependence. Meanwhile, these obtained differences, with average deviations around 0.6%, are small if experimental uncertainty is considered. Maximum discrepancies of 1.6% with regard to Maxwell [46] and the derived solution for Bruggeman equations [49] for the 5.0 wt% mass concentration are obtained. As it is known, the underlying mechanism that explains the increase in thermal conductivity in all nanofluids remains unclear to date. However, several potential contributions have been identified at the submicron scale, such as the liquid layering at the particle − fluid interface, Brownian motion of the nanoparticles, fluid convection at the microscales, cluster agglomeration, or a combination of mentioned mechanisms [52]. A growing body of literature identifies that the conduction path through the agglomerates is one of the most critical factor [53]. Certainly, it is possible to ascribe the formation of clusters of ZrO2 nanoparticles from the TEM characterization (see Fig. 1a) which would lead to the establishment of local percolation networks that constitute paths of high thermal conductivity [54]. This phenomenon is more pronounced as the concentration of nanoparticles increases while aggregation is not considered in the aforementioned models. Therefore, it could explain the superior observed discrepancies between experimental and theoretical values at high nanoparticle loadings.

The rheological characterization of the base fluid, as well as of ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids, was carried out and dynamic viscosities were measured at different temperatures between 283.15 and 323.15 K. First, non-linear viscoelastic experiments were performed to analyse the shear viscosity, η, with increasing shear rate,
γ ̇
. The resulting flow curves at 283.15 K of the studied nanofluids and base fluid are depicted in Fig. 7 as an example. The well-known Newtonian behaviour of the PG:W 10:90 vol% mixture was evidenced and also observed the same nature for all the analysed concentrations of ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids.

Once the Newtonian behaviour was evidenced, constant shear rate tests at 100 s−1 were carried out to determine the dynamic viscosity of PG:W 10:90 vol% mixture within the temperature range from 283.15 to 323.15 K with a 10 K temperature step. The obtained experimental data was compared to that reported in the literature by Melinder [28] and ASHRAE [32] finding maximum deviations of 3.7 and 2.7%, respectively. All the departures are under the aforementioned device uncertainty (k = 2) of 5%, as it is shown in Fig. 8 a. In addition, it is important to highlight that there is a disagreement in the literature data from Melinder and ASHRAE at 283.15 K, dynamic viscosity values of the reported references differing 11% from each other. This outcome explains the 13.2% deviation we found between Melinder and this work at 283.15 K, which has not been considered in the data depicted in Fig. 8a.

Afterwards, dynamic viscosities of all ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids were experimentally determined in the same temperature range. Fig. 8b shows higher dynamic viscosity values with the dispersion of ZrO2 nanoparticles, obtaining average increases from 1.8% at 0.25 wt% to 13% at 5.0 wt% in comparison to the base fluid. The addition of ZrO2 particles into the base fluid causes an increase of its friction and flow resistance, leading to higher viscosity values as the nanoparticle concentration increases [24]. Regarding the temperature dependence, a decreasing trend of dynamic viscosity was found in the studied temperature range with increasing temperature for base fluid and all studied nanofluids; decreases ranging from around 60% for the PG:W 10:90 vol% and 0.25 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid to 56% for the 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid. This behaviour was expected due to the weakening of the intermolecular adhesion forces as reported in the literature [55–57] with increasing temperature. It can be observed that this decrease of dynamic viscosity values is more pronounced for the base fluid and the lowest concentration of ZrO2. This effect is due to the increased amounts of solid nanoparticles dispersed in the fluid that start to agglomerate and increase the viscosity of the nanofluid.

Dynamic viscosities results were also fitted through the Vogel-Fulcher-Tammann equation [58–60] that is one of the most widespread equations in the literature to describe the temperature dependence of the viscosity and it can be expressed as follows:
(6)

where

the Vogel divergence temperature. These parameters were fitted to the experimental data for both base fluid and nanofluids and are shown in Table 2 . The
η 0
and
T 0
increase with increasing nanoparticle loading while
D T
decreases. Thus, larger ZrO2 loadings entail higher viscosity, higher nanofluid glass transition temperatures and higher fragility of the dispersion structure due to the intermolecular interactions between the nanoparticles. Deviations of the experimental dynamic viscosity of nanofluids and that obtained through the Vogel-Fulcher-Tammann equation, are lower than 2.1% for the studied temperatures.

Densities of the base fluid as well as for all ZrO2 nanofluids were also measured in the temperature range from 288.15 to 313.15 K. Density increases with nanoparticle loading from 0.20% for the 0.25 wt% nanofluid to 4.1% for 5.0 wt% nanofluid were observed compared to the base fluid and are depicted in Fig. 9 a. Regarding the temperature dependence, density decreases with rising temperature of around 0.8% for all samples in the studied temperature range, which suggests that the dispersion of nanoparticles has no effect on the change of density with increasing temperature. However, a linear increase of the nanofluid density with the nanoparticle loading was observed, as reported in the literature [61–63]. Thus, the mixture rule-based expression first used by Pak and Cho [64] for nanofluids that described this relationship and estimates density considering nanofluids simple solid–liquid mixtures was employed to obtain the nanoparticle density by using experimental data of 0.25 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid. The reliability of this equation was also confirmed [65] obtaining differences between the experimental and the theoretical values in the range 0.01–0.9%. The mixture rule-based equation for the density of nanofluids is expressed as follows:

Isobaric heat capacities of ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids were experimentally measured as well as the ones corresponding to the base fluid in a temperature range from 283.15 to 333.15 K. All nanofluids show lower heat capacities than the base fluid, reaching a maximum decrease of 11.2 % for the 5.0 wt% concentration of ZrO2 nanoparticles. This decreasing trend is expected due to lower heat capacity of the nanoparticles (
c
p , p
= ∼0.442 J g−1 K−1 [66]) with regard to the base fluid (
c
p , b f
= ∼4.085 J g−1 K−1 from the experimental measurements in this work).

Slight increases with the rising temperature that range from 1.4% for the base fluid to 3.8% for the 5.0 wt% nanofluid were also found, as it can be observed in Fig. 10 a. Regarding the effect of the nanoparticle loading, heat capacity ratios are depicted in Fig. 10b. Vajjha and Das [67] equation was used to correlate the heat capacities of the studied nanofluids that is expressed as follows:
(6)

It can be appreciated that Vajjha and Das equation adjusts low mass concentration data more precisely than those at higher mass concentration of ZrO2, showing minimum absolute average deviation (AAD%) values of 0.05% for 0.25 ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids and up to 1.9% for 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids, being 0.5% the average for all mass concentrations of ZrO2 in this work. These deviations agree with those by Cabaleiro et al. [66] that compared experimental heat capacity data of MgO, ZnO, and ZrO2 nanofluids based on pure ethylene glycol (EG) and ethylene glycol:water (EG:W) 50:50 vol% mixture at different mass concentrations (2.5, 5, 10, and 15 wt%). It seems that the type of base fluid has an influence on the obtained AAD% values, since the deviations ranged from 0.5 to 0.71% for ZrO2/EG, ZrO2/EG:W 50:50 vol%, and ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids. Likewise, the type of nanopowder might also contribute to obtaining AAD% values within a specific range. Different results were obtained dispersing MgO, ZrO2, or ZnO in the same base fluid [66]. However, more studies comparing experimental heat capacity data with those obtained by Vajjha and Das equation are needed to conclude if the obtained AAD% values are related to the type of nanoparticle or the base fluid.

Five different loaded ZrO2 nanofluids (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, and 5.0 wt%) based on a PG:W 10:90 vol% binary mixture were designed and extensively characterized through their thermophysical and rheological profiles in this study. The nanopowder was initially analysed by means of various important parameters and features.

Pseudo-spherical shape of the nanopowder and mean particle size of 26 */2 nm was observed. The crystallinity of the nanoparticles corresponded to monoclinic and tetragonal structures, being the last one the predominant. FTIR spectrum showed no chemical bonds between the nanoparticles and the base fluid but between the base fluid molecules themselves. In addition, the higher the mass concentration of nanoparticles, the weaker the hydrogen bonding.

Slight enhancements were found with the nanoparticle loading in thermal conductivity, 2.8% being the highest average enhancement found for the 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid. Regarding the rheological analysis, Newtonian behaviour was found for all ZrO2/PG:W nanofluids. As expected, dynamic viscosity increases with the nanoparticle loading, finding average increments of 13% for the 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid while decreases up to 60% were found for the base fluid and ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids with the increasing temperature. Density increases and isobaric heat capacity decreases up to 4.1% and 11.2%, respectively, for the 5.0 wt% ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluid were observed in comparison to the base fluid. The increments in thermal conductivity of ZrO2/PG:W 10:90 vol% nanofluids are not as high as others obtained in the literature, for example, for graphene nanoplatelet-based nanofluids. However, dynamic viscosity increases are much lower with the dispersion of ZrO2 nanoparticles than with the aforementioned carbon-based nanoadditives in comparison to the base fluid [24]. Moderate increases in dynamic viscosity are convenient for a possible working fluid in heat transfer applications since it would not compromise the pumping power of the installation. Therefore, based on the experimental results obtained, ZrO2 nanofluids could be a potential working fluid for low-enthalpy GHP systems. Likewise, further determinations as heat transfer coefficients and pressure drops analysis need be performed to truly determine the feasibility of the studied nanofluids as geothermal fluids.
